title: Wrap repr(value) and error formatting in defensive try/catch
description: EDGE-001/EDGE-002: repr(value) at src/middleware/core.jl:74 and showerror() in error formatting can themselves throw (e.g., broken show() method, type with no repr). This kills the eval handler with no error response to the client. Fix: wrap repr(value) in try/catch with fallback to string(typeof(value)) or a safe '<repr failed>' placeholder. Wrap showerror() similarly. Files: src/middleware/core.jl:74 (repr call), src/middleware/core.jl:80-85 (error formatting). Done when: a test evals an expression whose result has a broken show() method, and the response contains a fallback repr string instead of crashing.
close_reason: Fix was already applied in a prior session along with matching tests. All 898 tests pass.

title: Use Meta.parseall for multi-expression eval
description: CORR-002: Meta.parse at src/middleware/core.jl:54 only handles the first expression in multi-line code. Input like 'x = 1\ny = x + 1' silently drops everything after the first expression. Fix: replace Meta.parse(code) with Meta.parseall(code) and eval all expressions, returning the result of the last one (matching REPL semantics). Files: src/middleware/core.jl:54 (parse call). Done when: a test sends 'x = 1\ny = x + 1' and receives y=2 as the result, confirming both expressions were evaluated.
close_reason: Fix was already applied in a prior session along with matching tests. All 898 tests pass.

title: Add try/catch in handle_client! for connection-level error recovery
description: CORR-004: handle_client! at src/transport/tcp.jl:31 has no try/catch around handler(msg). Any unexpected throw kills the client connection silently with no error response. Fix: wrap the handler(msg) call in try/catch that returns a JSON-RPC error response to the client instead of dropping the connection. Files: src/transport/tcp.jl:31 (handle_client!), src/errors.jl (error response formatting). Done when: a test sends a request that causes an internal throw, and the client receives a proper JSON-RPC error response instead of a disconnection.
close_reason: Fix was already applied in a prior session along with matching tests. All 898 tests pass.

title: Replace process-global redirect_stdout/stderr with per-task capture
description: CORR-001: EvalMiddleware at src/middleware/core.jl:49-58 uses process-global redirect_stdout/redirect_stderr. Concurrent evals corrupt each other's captured output. Fix: Replace redirect_stdout/stderr with IOCapture.jl (add as dependency). IOCapture.capture() returns (value, output) per-eval without touching global state. Files: src/middleware/core.jl:49-58 (redirect logic), Project.toml (add IOCapture dep). Done when: two concurrent eval requests each capture only their own stdout, verified by a test that evals 'println(x)' in parallel and asserts outputs don't cross-contaminate.

title: COMP-1: InterruptMiddleware and StdinMiddleware bypass ctx.session, re-resolve from manager with TOCTOU window
description: InterruptMiddleware (src/middleware/interrupt.jl:43-50) and StdinMiddleware (src/middleware/stdin.jl:44-56) each re-read the 'session' field from the raw message and call lookup_named_session(ctx.manager, ...) directly, bypassing ctx.session that SessionMiddleware already resolved. This creates: (1) a TOCTOU window where a concurrent destroy_named_session! between SessionMiddleware resolution and the second lookup causes spurious session-not-found errors; (2) incomplete validation — the weaker isempty check is used instead of validate_session_name; (3) non-idempotent composition — adding SessionMiddleware does not guarantee downstream middleware will use ctx.session. Fix: InterruptMiddleware and StdinMiddleware should consume ctx.session (a NamedSession when SessionMiddleware ran) and only fall back to a lookup when ctx.session is not a NamedSession.
close_reason: ctx.session used directly when NamedSession; fallback lookup only when not set

title: DX: Docs teach unexported symbols as primary API (SessionManager, create_named_session!, EvalMiddleware, SessionMiddleware)
description: Multiple documentation pages use 'using REPLy: X' syntax for symbols that are NOT exported from the REPLy module: SessionManager, create_named_session!, EvalMiddleware, SessionMiddleware, session_name. Files: docs/src/howto-sessions.md (lines 33, 168, 184), docs/src/index.md (line 102), docs/src/howto-mcp-adapter.md (lines 47, 70, 133). While Julia's 'using M: X' syntax works for non-exported symbols, the docs present these as canonical integration patterns without disclosing that users depend on internals. This creates fragile integrations that break on refactoring without deprecation contract. Resolution: either (a) add these symbols to the public export list in src/REPLy.jl, or (b) rewrite user-facing docs to use higher-level patterns that don't require direct SessionManager construction. Finding L-1 from REPLy_jl-umg.12.

title: perf: EVAL_IO_CAPTURE_LOCK serialises all concurrent evals to single-thread throughput
description: EVAL_IO_CAPTURE_LOCK (eval.jl:40) wraps the entire eval body including Core.eval() because redirect_stdout(IOStream) uses dup2 (process-global fd replacement). This means all concurrent eval requests — up to max_concurrent_evals=10 — must wait behind a single global lock. Actual eval concurrency is 1 regardless of Julia thread count or client connections. This is the dominant throughput bottleneck in the server. The fix requires a per-eval fd isolation mechanism (e.g., per-task pipe pairs, process-level workaround, or operating without IO capture for non-interactive evals). Candidate benchmark: spawn N tasks with trivial eval and measure throughput vs N.
notes: Investigation 2026-04-30: redirect_stdout with any IO object that has a real fd (IOStream, Pipe) uses dup2 in Julia 1.12 — confirmed via testing. redirect_stdout(IOBuffer()) throws MethodError. Task-local stdout (task_local_storage(:STDOUT, buf)) does not intercept print(). The lock is necessary for correctness. Fixing dxp requires either: (1) a Julia runtime feature for per-task fd redirection, (2) running evals in subprocesses, or (3) redesigning to not capture stdout globally. This is not fixable at the Julia 1.10-1.12 stdlib level without major architectural change.

title: perf: replace mktemp IO capture with pipe-based capture in _run_eval_core
description: Every eval call creates two temporary files via mktemp() for stdout/stderr capture (eval.jl:70-71). This is a filesystem round-trip on every eval hot-path involving kernel syscalls (open, fstat, unlink). For a server processing many short evals, this introduces avoidable syscall overhead and filesystem latency. Replace with a pipe-based (libuv) in-memory capture mechanism. Note: redirect_stdout/stderr require an IOStream-backed fd, so a Base.Pipe or similar libuv-backed pipe is needed rather than a bare IOBuffer. Candidate benchmark: microbenchmark _run_eval_core with 1+1 vs pipe implementation.

title: SEC: Revise hook executes caller-controlled Main.Revise.revise() (H-5)
description: _maybe_revise! calls Main.Revise.revise() before each named-session eval with no validation that Main.Revise is the real Revise package. An attacker who can eval 'module Revise; revise()=<payload>; end' in any eval then has code executed before every subsequent named-session eval. This is a process-level persistence and lateral-movement primitive within the server. Validation: eval 'module Revise; revise()=error("injected"); end' then run another named-session eval; confirm hook fires. Severity: High.

title: SEC: stdin_channel is unbounded Channel{String}(Inf) — heap exhaustion (H-3)
description: NamedSession.stdin_channel is Channel{String}(Inf) — an unbounded buffered channel. An attacker sends thousands of op=stdin messages to a named session faster than any eval can consume them. Each message is a heap-allocated String. No rate limit applies specifically to stdin ops vs other ops. With rate_limit_per_min=600 (default) and a session that is idle, 600 stdin messages/min accumulate indefinitely. Severity: High.

title: SEC: readline() pre-allocates oversized message before size check (H-2)
description: In protocol/message.jl receive(), readline() buffers the full message in memory before the size check fires (documented in code comment). Sending a 256MiB newline-free payload causes Julia to allocate the full string before MessageTooLargeError is raised. Under concurrent connections this enables heap exhaustion. A true streaming reader with early abort is needed. Severity: High.

title: SEC: No TCP connection count limit — connection exhaustion (H-1)
description: The TCP accept loop accepts connections indefinitely with no maximum concurrent connection count. Each connection consumes a Julia Task, an IO handle, and OS file descriptors. Rate limiting is per-connection, not global, so this bypasses rate limits trivially. Severity: High.

title: SEC: eval 'module' field allows targeting Main and system modules (C-3)
description: The 'module' field in eval requests allows targeting any module reachable from Main, including Main itself, Base, Core, and any library loaded in the process. An attacker can inject bindings into shared module namespaces, bypassing session isolation. resolve_module walks from Main down, so an attacker who names a binding to shadow a module path can also redirect module resolution. Validation: send eval with code='x_test=42' and module='Main'; confirm Main.x_test==42 in server. Severity: Critical.

title: LookupMiddleware._lookup_symbol executes arbitrary user input via Core.eval (RCE)
description: src/middleware/lookup.jl _lookup_symbol calls Core.eval(module_, Meta.parse(symbol_str)) where symbol_str is user-supplied. A client sending symbol_str='run(`rm -rf /`)' executes arbitrary code in the server process. The try/catch silently returns found=>false masking the execution. Fix: validate symbol_str as a valid Julia identifier (not arbitrary expression) before parsing, or use isdefined+getfield instead of Core.eval. This is a remote code execution vulnerability when LookupMiddleware is in the stack.

title: SEC: load-file reads arbitrary files — no default allowlist (C-2)
description: LoadFileMiddleware with load_file_allowlist=nothing (the shipped default) allows reading any file the server process can access. An attacker can send op=load-file with file=/etc/shadow, /proc/self/environ, SSH keys, etc. and receive file contents back. No path normalization is performed. Validation: send op=load-file with file=/proc/self/environ; confirm content returned. Severity: Critical.

title: SEC: No authentication on TCP eval endpoint (C-1)
description: Any client that can reach the TCP port (or Unix socket) can execute arbitrary Julia code in the server process with no credential check. There is no token, certificate, session cookie, or any other barrier before code executes. On multi-user systems or when host is set to a non-loopback address, this is a full remote code execution with no barrier. Validation: connect unauthenticated, send op=eval with code=run(`id`), confirm shell output in response. Severity: Critical.

title: readline() in receive() allocates unbounded memory before size check fires (OOM DoS)
description: src/protocol/message.jl comment documents this: readline() buffers the full message in memory before the size check fires. A client sending a multi-GB newline-free stream exhausts heap before MessageTooLargeError is raised. Fix: replace readline() with a bounded streaming byte reader that enforces max_message_bytes at the read level, not after the fact. This is a correctness/security issue traceable to the initial OOM-safe design assumption.
close_reason: Fixed in security(phase-1) commit 2b2a638: readline() was replaced with read_bounded_line() which bounds allocation to max_message_bytes before the size check fires

title: Fix: clone registration races with binding copy (IS-7)
description:
Why: In clone_named_session!, the destination clone is inserted into named_sessions before the binding-copy loop finishes. An exception mid-copy leaves a partially-initialized session discoverable in the registry, violating the invariant that all registry entries are fully initialized.

Scope: Use a two-phase pattern — copy bindings privately, publish to registry atomically only on success.

Found during: REPLy_jl-umg.10 formal-verification readiness pass.
File: src/session/manager.jl (clone_named_session!)
close_reason: Two-phase commit: build dest privately, copy bindings, publish atomically. Regression test uses custom _FailCopy6fh type to verify partial sessions never enter registry.

title: Fix: close vs running eval race — close does not acquire eval_lock (HAZ-1)
description:
Why: handle_close_session and destroy_named_session! remove a session from the registry without acquiring eval_lock. If an eval is running or queued, it continues on a now-detached session object. This is the highest-risk lifecycle race, blocking formal verification of the close semantics specification.

Scope: Acquire eval_lock before transitioning to SessionClosed. Document close as blocking until all in-flight evals on the session complete, then marking the session terminal.

Found during: REPLy_jl-umg.10 formal-verification readiness pass.
Files: src/middleware/session_ops.jl (handle_close_session), src/session/manager.jl (destroy_named_session!)

title: Fix: active_eval_tasks bookkeeping leak on invalid module path (IS-6)
description:
Why: In eval_responses, when the module path is invalid, active_evals is decremented but active_eval_tasks is not cleaned up. This leaves stale task entries in the server-wide bookkeeping and violates the invariant that active_eval_tasks and active_evals are always in sync.

Scope: Move module path resolution before task registration so the registration/unregistration pair is always inside a single finally block.

Found during: REPLy_jl-umg.10 formal-verification readiness pass.
File: src/middleware/eval.jl (eval_responses)
close_reason: Resolved: move module path resolution before concurrency registration so invalid path returns before active_evals/active_eval_tasks are touched.

title: Fix: alias replacement creates detached session objects (IS-3)
description:
Why: create_named_session! marks the old session Closed and removes it from the registry when a same-alias replacement occurs, but callers holding references to the old object can still mutate its state (bindings, stdin_channel). This blocks formalization of the alias/UUID contract (INV-2) and creates race conditions.

Scope: Decide between (A) disallow same-name replacement—throw error if alias exists, (B) make replacement explicit and destructive with documentation, or (C) acquire eval_lock before close. Recommendation: Option A or B.

Found during: REPLy_jl-umg.10 formal-verification readiness pass.
File: src/session/manager.jl (create_named_session!)
close_reason: create_named_session! now throws ArgumentError on duplicate alias (Option A). Detached-reference hazard eliminated. handle_new_session catches ArgumentError and returns error response. Tests updated.

title: Fix sessions how-to: client-callable RPC ops undocumented, server-side-only constraint is false
description:
The sessions how-to states that creating named sessions must be done from the server side in Julia. This is incorrect — the implementation has a fully client-callable new-session RPC op (src/middleware/session_ops.jl). Clients can also list, clone, and close sessions at runtime.

What needs to be fixed:
- Remove the false constraint from the how-to intro
- Document all four client RPC ops (new-session, ls-sessions, clone, close) with examples
- Cross-link Quick Start to sessions how-to so readers discover sessions exist
- Clarify that create_named_session! is a server-side seed-session helper, not the primary interface
close_reason: Fixed in commit 89922d6 — docs already updated: client-callable RPC ops (new-session, ls-sessions, clone, close) documented, server-side constraint corrected, Quick Start cross-link added

title: Fix close request schema to match spec
description:
Why this exists: The spec defines close as: request={'op':'close','session':'<uuid>'}. The current impl uses {'op':'close-session','name':'<string>'}. What needs to be done: (1) Accept 'session' field (UUID or name) as the session to close. (2) Update close e2e tests to use spec-compliant schema. Depends on: hybrid session identity issue and op-name fix.

Source files: src/middleware/session_ops.jl (close handler).

title: Fix clone request/response schema to match spec
description:
Why this exists: The spec defines clone as: request={'op':'clone','session':'<uuid>','type':'light'}, response={'new-session':'<uuid>'}. The current impl uses {'op':'clone-session','source':'<name>','name':'<new-name>'} with response {'name':'<string>'}. What needs to be done: (1) Accept 'session' field (UUID or name) as the source session identifier. (2) Accept 'type' field — 'light' only for now. If 'type':'heavy' is sent, return {'status':'not-supported','message':'heavy sessions are post-v1.0'}. (3) Return 'new-session' as the UUID of the cloned session (plus 'name' if alias was provided). (4) Update clone e2e tests to use spec-compliant schema. Depends on: hybrid session identity issue.

Source files: src/middleware/session_ops.jl (clone handler).

title: Hybrid session identity: UUID canonical + optional name alias
description:
Why this exists: The spec uses opaque server-generated UUIDs as session identity. The current implementation uses user-supplied string names. To support both interactive use (human-readable names) and programmatic/MCP use (stable opaque IDs), we adopt Option C: UUIDs are canonical, names are optional aliases. What needs to be done: (1) Add UUID generation to new-session — generate a UUID on session creation. (2) Add name→UUID lookup table to ServerState. (3) Update new-session response to return both 'session' (UUID) and 'name' (alias, if provided). (4) Update all session operations (eval, interrupt, clone, close, ls-sessions) to accept either UUID or name as the session identifier. (5) Update e2e tests to exercise both identity forms. This is the foundational blocker for all other spec alignment issues.

Response contract: request={'op':'new-session','name':'<optional-alias>'}, response={'session':'<uuid>','name':'<alias|null>','status':'ok'}. All subsequent ops accept either the UUID or the name alias interchangeably.

Atomicity note: If the ServerState struct change is complex, consider splitting into (a) data model change only — add UUID + name→UUID map (structural, no behavior change), then (b) update routing/responses. Either way, this is the gate for all dependents.

Source files: src/config/server_state.jl (session struct + name→UUID map), src/session/manager.jl (session creation), src/middleware/session_ops.jl (new-session handler), src/server.jl (request routing).

title: Expand ls-sessions response to include all spec-required fields
description:
Why this exists: The spec requires ls-sessions to return per-session: id (UUID), type (light/heavy), created (ISO timestamp), last-activity (ISO timestamp), module (module name), eval-count (integer), pid (process pid for heavy sessions). The current implementation returns only 'name' and 'created-at'. What needs to be done: (1) Add UUID field to session state (prerequisite: hybrid identity issue). (2) Track last-activity timestamp on every eval completion. (3) Track eval-count per session. (4) Track module name — use 'Main' for the primary session module, '<anonymous>' for ephemeral/one-shot sessions, and the Malt worker module name for heavy sessions. (5) Add pid field (null for light sessions, worker pid for heavy). (6) Update ls-sessions handler to return all required fields. (7) Update e2e tests.

Source files: src/config/server_state.jl (session struct fields), src/session/manager.jl (session tracking), src/middleware/session_ops.jl (ls-sessions handler).

title: Fix operation names: clone-session→clone, close-session→close
description:
Why this exists: The OpenSpec protocol specifies op names 'clone' and 'close'. The current implementation uses 'clone-session' and 'close-session'. This breaks protocol interoperability with spec-compliant clients. What needs to be done: Rename the operation dispatch in the server handler so that 'clone' and 'close' are the canonical op names. Keep 'clone-session' and 'close-session' as deprecated aliases (with a deprecation log warning) to avoid breaking existing clients. Remove aliases when the MCP adapter ships and all integration tests use spec-compliant op names — that is the exit condition, not a version number.

Source files: src/middleware/session_ops.jl (op dispatch table), src/middleware/core.jl (op routing).

title: Spec alignment phase 10: session identity, protocol ops, field coverage
description: Why this exists: An audit of the OpenSpec protocol spec against the current implementation revealed significant divergences across session identity (UUIDs vs names), operation names, request/response schemas, and ls-sessions field coverage. This epic tracks all remediation work needed to achieve full spec compliance while preserving the named-session UX via a hybrid identity model. What needs to be done: Complete all child issues under this epic to reach spec compliance.
close_reason: All 6 leaf issues complete — awg, xkg, ojy, db4, 4ye, 0hl, 4kf, l4b

title: Add ReentrantLock to SessionManager for thread safety
description: CORR-006: SessionManager at src/session/manager.jl has no synchronization. Concurrent create/close/lookup operations can corrupt the sessions dictionaries. Fix: add a ReentrantLock field to SessionManager and wrap all mutation operations (create_named_session!, destroy_named_session!, create_session!, close_session!) in lock() blocks. Read operations (lookup, list, session_count) should also lock for consistency. Cross-ref: overlaps with REPLy_jl-cfr (lifecycle race coverage) — this ticket adds the lock, cfr adds the comprehensive race condition tests. Files: src/session/manager.jl (all mutation functions). Done when: concurrent create/destroy calls from multiple tasks do not corrupt state, verified by a test spawning 10 tasks that simultaneously create and destroy sessions.
close_reason: Added ReentrantLock to SessionManager; all mutations and reads wrapped; clone_named_session! holds lock only for dict operations; concurrency test added

title: Document and validate ignored fields in mcp_eval_request
description: CORR-005: mcp_eval_request at src/mcp_adapter.jl:96 accepts fields (timeout, module) that are silently ignored. Fix: add validation that returns a 'not yet supported' error if these fields are provided, rather than silently ignoring them. This prevents user confusion without removing the fields from the schema. Cross-ref: overlaps with REPLy_jl-1le.1 which will implement these fields — at that point, remove the validation guard. Files: src/mcp_adapter.jl:96-120 (request handler). Done when: a test sends a request with a timeout field and receives a clear error saying the field is not yet supported.
close_reason: Added not-yet-supported guards for module and timeout_ms in mcp_eval_request; removed pass-through, updated tests

title: Add stub responses for unimplemented MCP tools
description: DRAFT-004: MCP adapter (src/mcp_adapter.jl:17-86) advertises julia_complete, julia_lookup, julia_load_file, and julia_interrupt which have no middleware implementation yet. Rather than removing (they have planned implementation tickets: 95q.1-3, 0gk.1), add stub handlers that return a structured 'not yet implemented' error response. Done when: calling each unimplemented tool returns a proper JSON-RPC error with code and message rather than falling through to UnknownOpMiddleware.
close_reason: Added mcp_stub_result() function returning not-yet-implemented error for julia_complete/lookup/load_file/interrupt; test added to mcp_adapter_test.jl

title: Handle Module type in clone_named_session! deepcopy
description: CORR-003: deepcopy at src/session/manager.jl:129 throws on Module-typed values. Modules are mutable so the ismutable(val) check passes, causing deepcopy to be called on them. Fix: add 'val isa Module && continue' before the deepcopy call at manager.jl:129. Done when: test creates a named session that imports a module (e.g. Base), clones it via clone_named_session!, and verifies the clone has the module binding without throwing.
close_reason: Added val isa Module && continue guard at manager.jl:129; test added to session_registry_test.jl

title: Fix tracer-bullet output capture deadlock and align output contract
description:
## Context
Rule-of-5 review found a confirmed deadlock in eval output capture for large stdout/stderr, plus a contract mismatch between docs/tests saying "streaming/chunks" and the implementation aggregating buffered output after eval completes.

## Files
- src/middleware/core.jl
- test/unit/eval_middleware_test.jl
- test/integration/pipeline_test.jl
- .wai/projects/tracer-bullet/designs/2026-04-17-testing-validation-strategy-principle-o.md
- .wai/projects/tracer-bullet/research/2026-04-17-tracer-bullet-scope-a-tracer-bullet-cuts-throu.md

## Done
- [ ] Eval output capture cannot deadlock on large stdout/stderr.
- [ ] Tests cover large-output completion and the chosen output-order contract.
- [ ] Docs/tests/code agree on buffered-vs-streaming semantics.
- [ ] just --justfile justfile test passes.

## Workflow Mandate
- Add failing tests first.
- Keep behavior focused on the tracer-bullet contract; avoid unrelated transport/session changes.

title: Expand integration pipeline coverage for tracer-bullet contract
description:
## Context
Plan section: integration-layer expectations and coverage rows for stdout ordering, unknown op, parse error, eval error, and ephemeral cleanup in `.wai/projects/tracer-bullet/designs/2026-04-17-testing-validation-strategy-principle-o.md`.

The current integration test exercises only the happy path, but the tracer-bullet design expects integration coverage for stdout ordering, unknown op, parse error, eval error, and ephemeral-session cleanup.

This task depends on streamed output support landing first so the integration assertions can reflect the intended contract.

## Files
- test/integration/pipeline_test.jl
- test/helpers/conformance.jl
- src/middleware/core.jl
- src/session/

## Done
- [ ] Integration tests cover success with streamed output, unknown op, parse error, eval error, and ephemeral-session leak detection.
- [ ] Tests assert protocol conformance and session cleanup explicitly.
- [ ] Tests assert `session_count(manager) == 0` before and after ephemeral eval flows.
- [ ] Scenario assertions cover expected value/error shape and status flags where applicable.
- [ ] `just --justfile justfile test` passes.

## Workflow Mandate
- Add failing integration tests first.
- Reuse existing pipeline/session APIs rather than adding new abstractions unless strictly required.
- This issue owns cross-middleware integration behavior and leak detection, not unit-level output chunk semantics.

title: Implement streamed stdout/stderr messages for eval responses
description:
## Context
Plan section: stdout/stderr capture, streaming-order expectations, and eval middleware coverage in `.wai/projects/tracer-bullet/designs/2026-04-17-testing-validation-strategy-principle-o.md`, plus tracer-bullet scope item `Core-operations — Core.eval in session module, stdout/stderr capture, value repr` in `.wai/projects/tracer-bullet/research/2026-04-17-tracer-bullet-scope-a-tracer-bullet-cuts-throu.md`.

The tracer-bullet implementation currently returns only value+done from eval requests, but the documented thin slice expects streamed stdout/stderr chunks before the terminal value.

## Files
- src/middleware/core.jl
- src/errors.jl
- test/unit/eval_middleware_test.jl
- test/runtests.jl
- test/helpers/conformance.jl

## Done
- [ ] Eval requests emit stdout/stderr chunks before value and done when code writes to those streams.
- [ ] Empty-code and error-path behavior remains shape-compatible with the existing protocol.
- [ ] A dedicated eval middleware unit test file covers stdout capture, stderr capture, empty code, streaming order, and err-field disambiguation.
- [ ] `just --justfile justfile test` passes.

## Workflow Mandate
- First red step: create `test/unit/eval_middleware_test.jl` and include it from `test/runtests.jl`.
- Start from failing unit tests for eval middleware.
- Keep scope limited to the tracer-bullet eval path; do not add timeouts, stdin, or new operations.
- This issue owns unit-level output chunk semantics and ordering.

title: Fix handler validation and eval session ownership
description:
## Context
Rule-of-5 review of the new tracer-bullet middleware found two correctness bugs in the freshly landed code.

`build_handler` dispatches requests directly into middleware without calling `validate_request`, so malformed requests are misclassified as `unknown-op`. Also, `EvalMiddleware` creates a fallback ephemeral session when `ctx.session` is `nothing` but never destroys it, which leaks sessions for custom middleware stacks.

## Done
- [ ] `build_handler` validates requests before middleware dispatch and preserves protocol error shapes for malformed input.
- [ ] Eval session ownership is explicit and leak-free for both the default stack and custom middleware stacks.
- [ ] Regression tests cover malformed requests through `build_handler` and session-count stability for custom eval stacks.
- [ ] `just test` passes.

## Notes
This is a bounded bug-fix pass on the newly added tracer-bullet middleware code, not a broad refactor.

title: fix: find_first_paragraph leaks code-fence content into handoff snippet
description: find_first_paragraph() in prime.rs does not track code-fence state. When handoff sections are empty (HTML comment placeholders only), it falls through to the Context section and picks up git-status lines from inside a triple-backtick block. Reproducer: wai prime in REPLy.jl shows RESUMING with 'M  .beads/issues.jsonl' instead of 'no summary yet'. Fix: toggle in_code_block on backtick-fence lines and skip their content.

title: Wire TCP transport for tracer-bullet acceptance path
description:
## Context
Plan section: TDD progression step 10 and tracer-bullet scope item "Target: eval over TCP" from `.wai/projects/tracer-bullet/research/2026-04-17-tracer-bullet-scope-a-tracer-bullet-cuts-throu.md`.

Implement the TCP listener/server lifecycle so a real socket client can send a newline-delimited `eval` request and receive the expected value+done stream.

## Files
- `src/transport/tcp.jl`
- `src/server.jl`
- `src/REPLy.jl`
- `test/helpers/tcp_client.jl`
- `test/helpers/server.jl`
- `test/e2e/eval_test.jl`

## Done
- [ ] `test/e2e/eval_test.jl` passes for the definitive acceptance test: send `{"op":"eval","id":"1","code":"1+1"}` over TCP and receive `value` then `done`.
- [ ] The server can bind with `port=0` and exposes the assigned port to tests.
- [ ] Shutdown happens reliably in `finally` paths so tests do not leak sockets/tasks.
- [ ] `just test` passes for unit + integration + the main e2e acceptance path.

## Workflow Mandate
- Begin with the failing end-to-end acceptance test.
- Keep the transport limited to TCP; Unix sockets and other listeners remain deferred.
- Avoid speculative abstraction beyond what the tracer-bullet path exercises.
notes: Implemented a minimal TCP tracer-bullet server: serve(port=0) binds an OS-assigned port, spawns an accept loop plus per-client tasks, routes newline-delimited JSON requests through build_handler, and closes listener/client sockets in finally-safe shutdown paths. just test is now fully green, including the e2e eval-over-TCP acceptance path.

description:
## Context

## Files

title: DX: tutorial-custom-client.md misidentifies JSON3 as a standard library
description: docs/src/tutorial-custom-client.md line 20 states: 'We will use Julia's Sockets and JSON3 standard libraries.' Sockets is a Julia standard library. JSON3 is a third-party registered package (JSON3.jl) that must be added with Pkg.add("JSON3"). The tutorial never mentions this requirement. A developer following the tutorial would get: ERROR: ArgumentError: Package JSON3 not found in current path. Fix: change the prose to say 'Sockets standard library and the JSON3 package' and add a Pkg.add("JSON3") step. Finding L-5 from REPLy_jl-umg.12.

title: DX: howto-mcp-adapter.md never shows how to instantiate a JSONTransport — transport wiring is invisible
description: docs/src/howto-mcp-adapter.md shows collect_reply_stream(transport, request_id) and send(transport, request) but never shows how to create an AbstractTransport connected to a running REPLy server. The only way to discover this is to find JSONTransport in the API (not prominently documented) and construct it: conn = connect(ip"127.0.0.1", 5555); transport = JSONTransport(conn, ReentrantLock()). This construction is not in any doc. JSONTransport is exported but there is no example of instantiating one. Fix: add a transport construction snippet to howto-mcp-adapter.md before the eval section. Finding I-1 from REPLy_jl-umg.12.

title: DX: index.md middleware example silently degrades server — replaces full default stack with 2 elements
description: docs/src/index.md lines 100-108 shows a resource limits example that passes middleware=[SessionMiddleware(), EvalMiddleware(max_repr_bytes=100_000)] to serve(). This replaces the ENTIRE default middleware stack (which includes describe, session_ops, interrupt, load_file, complete, lookup, stdin, unknown_op middleware) with only two elements. The server would: return no response for any op other than eval (no unknown-op handler), silently fail all session lifecycle ops (new-session, ls-sessions, close, clone), have no interrupt support. There is no warning. Fix: either add a helper like serve(max_repr_bytes=N) that patches only EvalMiddleware in the default stack, or add an explicit warning admonition and show correct middleware composition syntax. Finding S-1 from REPLy_jl-umg.12.

title: DX: howto-mcp-adapter.md has no end-to-end example — julia_eval/mcp_call_tool split is buried in prose
description: docs/src/howto-mcp-adapter.md explains all the MCP adapter building blocks individually but never shows a working end-to-end integration. The critical architectural constraint — that julia_eval cannot go through mcp_call_tool because it requires a live transport — is buried in a single prose paragraph (line 63) and easy to miss. An integrator must mentally assemble: initialize → get tools → dispatch loop → for eval: create transport + mcp_eval_request + send + collect_reply_stream + reply_stream_to_mcp_result; for lifecycle: mcp_call_tool directly. Fix: add an end-to-end example section and elevate the eval/non-eval dispatch split to an architectural note or admonition box. Finding P-1 from REPLy_jl-umg.12.

title: COMP-6: MCP adapter bypasses SessionOpsMiddleware for session lifecycle, omitting session limit enforcement
description: mcp_call_tool (src/mcp_adapter.jl:217-241) calls SessionManager functions directly for julia_new_session, julia_list_sessions, and julia_close_session instead of routing through the middleware pipeline. The session limit check in SessionOpsMiddleware (lines 100-103) is not applied when julia_new_session is dispatched via mcp_call_tool, allowing the session limit to be exceeded via MCP but not via the direct protocol. This is a functional divergence, not just structural duplication. Fix: route MCP session lifecycle ops through the REPLy middleware pipeline, or extract the limit enforcement into a shared helper called by both paths.

title: DX: api.md is an autodoc stub with no prose — unusable without a rendered docs site
description: docs/src/api.md contains only a Documenter.jl @autodocs directive. Reading the raw file (GitHub browser, PR review) shows nothing useful. There is no link to a hosted rendered docs site in README.md or index.md. The public export surface in src/REPLy.jl is large (dozens of symbols) with no narrative distinguishing primary API from internal infrastructure. Without a rendered docs site or prose API reference, new integrators cannot discover the API surface. Fix: either (a) add prose API reference sections to api.md, or (b) set up and link to a hosted docs site (e.g. via Documenter.jl + GitHub Pages). Finding D-2 from REPLy_jl-umg.12.

title: COMP-3: Session limit check copy-pasted three times across session.jl and session_ops.jl
description: The session limit enforcement block (check !isnothing(ctx.server_state) && total_session_count >= limits.max_sessions, return error_response with 'session-limit-reached' flags) is inlined at: src/middleware/session.jl:53-58, src/middleware/session_ops.jl:100-103, src/middleware/session_ops.jl:225-228. Any change to error message, status flags, or limit semantics must be replicated in all three locations. Fix: extract a check_session_limit(ctx, request_id) -> Union{Nothing, Vector{Dict}} helper returning nothing when no limit is hit or an error response vector when the limit is exceeded.

title: DX: status.md capability matrix incorrectly shows Unix sockets as not implemented
description: docs/src/status.md capability matrix (line 33, 37, 53) states 'Unix socket and multi-listener transport' is not implemented (marked ❌). But src/server.jl fully implements Unix domain socket support via listen_unix() and the socket_path argument to serve(). server_socket_path() is also exported. docs/src/howto-unix-sockets.md documents the working feature in detail. The status.md was written during an earlier phase and never updated. Developers reading status.md to assess implementation maturity will incorrectly believe Unix socket support is absent. Fix: update the capability matrix and current implementation snapshot in status.md. Finding D-1 from REPLy_jl-umg.12.

title: COMP-2: Ephemeral session lifecycle and named-session eval serialization duplicated in eval.jl and load_file.jl
description: The ephemeral-fallback pattern (lines 248-253,388 of eval.jl; lines 60-61,78 of load_file.jl) and the named-session serialization block (eval.jl:303-337; load_file.jl:65-79) are copy-pasted verbatim across two files. Any change to the locking discipline, lifecycle transitions, or error messages must be replicated. eval.jl calls the ephemeral fallback a 'defensive fallback' that is effectively dead code with the default stack; load_file.jl duplicates it without that comment. Fix: introduce a with_session_eval(ctx, request_id, session; f::Function) -> Vector{Dict} helper that handles the full lifecycle. Both eval_responses and load_file_responses become callers.

title: DX: mcp_new_session_result docs show wrong content format — regex example crashes on real output
description: docs/src/howto-mcp-adapter.md line 52-54 shows: result["content"][1]["text"] == "Session: mcp-<uuid>". Line 139 shows a regex: match(r"Session: (mcp-\S+)", ...). But mcp_new_session_result() in src/mcp_adapter.jl line 165 produces: text_block("Session: $uuid") where uuid is a bare UUID4 string (e.g. 'f47ac10b-...'). The 'mcp-' prefix is NOT present. The regex r"Session: (mcp-\S+)" would return nothing from match(), and .captures[1] would throw a null-dereference error. Fix: correct the doc comment and regex example to match the actual output format. Finding L-4 from REPLy_jl-umg.12.

title: MOD-2: EVAL_IO_CAPTURE_LOCK shared implicitly between eval.jl and load_file.jl via include order
description: EVAL_IO_CAPTURE_LOCK is defined in src/middleware/eval.jl:40 and used directly in src/middleware/load_file.jl:87 with no explicit import or dependency declaration. The dependency is satisfied only because REPLy.jl includes eval.jl before load_file.jl (src/REPLy.jl:41,43). Reordering the includes would cause a runtime NameError. The shared lock is semantically correct (both redirect_stdout) but the coupling is invisible and undocumented. Fix: move EVAL_IO_CAPTURE_LOCK to a shared location (src/middleware/core.jl or a new eval_core.jl) and have both files reference it explicitly.
close_reason: EVAL_IO_CAPTURE_LOCK moved to core.jl; max_output_bytes applied to _run_load_file_core too

title: DX: mcp_ensure_default_session! docs say it returns name string but it returns UUID
description: docs/src/howto-mcp-adapter.md line 155 shows: session_name = mcp_ensure_default_session!(manager)  # "mcp-default". This implies the function returns the name string 'mcp-default'. But the actual implementation in src/mcp_adapter.jl returns session_id(session) — a UUID4 string. The source docstring correctly says 'returns the canonical UUID of the session'. Code using the documented pattern would assign a UUID to a variable and then try to use it as if it were 'mcp-default', causing routing confusion. Fix: correct the comment on that line to show the UUID return value, not the name alias. Finding L-3 from REPLy_jl-umg.12.

title: MOD-1: No abstract server handle type; TCPServerHandle/UnixServerHandle/MultiListenerServer shutdown logic duplicated
description: TCPServerHandle and UnixServerHandle (src/transport/tcp.jl:1-30) are structurally identical, differing only in listener type and path. MultiListenerServer (src/server.jl:182-225) re-implements the entire shutdown sequence verbatim as a loop over listeners — interrupt evals, wait for tasks, close clients, wait for client tasks, shutdown middleware, rm Unix paths — with no shared abstract function. A change to shutdown protocol must be applied in two diverging code paths. Fix: introduce AbstractServerHandle abstract type with a default close_server! implementation. MultiListenerServer delegates to it per-listener. Unix socket cleanup is the only specialization needed.

title: DX: howto-sessions.md error response examples omit 'done' flag — clients may loop forever
description: Section 5 and Section 6 of docs/src/howto-sessions.md show error responses WITHOUT the 'done' status flag. Example shown: {"status": ["error", "session-not-found"]}. But the actual session_not_found_response() and error_response() in src/errors.jl always prepend 'done' via unique(vcat(String["done"], status_flags)). Real response: {"status": ["done", "error", "session-not-found"]}. A client written following the doc examples would wait forever for a terminal 'done' after a session-not-found error, because the client logic (checking for 'done' to terminate the read loop) would see the done-containing response but not realize it was already terminal. Finding L-2 from REPLy_jl-umg.12.

title: perf: invokelatest world-age barrier on every named-session eval even when Revise is absent
description: _maybe_revise!() (eval.jl:197) calls Base.invokelatest(_revise_if_present) on every named-session eval when revise_hook_enabled=true (the default). The world-age barrier cost is paid before the isdefined(Main, :Revise) guard can short-circuit. For short evals (completions, lookups), this overhead is a non-trivial fraction of total latency. Consider caching whether Revise is present and only invoking invokelatest when Revise has been detected, or skipping invokelatest entirely when Revise was not loaded at server start.
notes: isdefined(Main,:Revise) fast path fails in test suite due to world-age interaction with Core.eval(Main,:(const Revise=mod)) injection — the fast path check at current world age returns false when the binding was created at a newer world. Since invokelatest overhead for short-circuit is sub-microsecond, deferred to post-1.0.

title: perf: receive() materialises JSON3.Object to Dict{String,Any} on every inbound message
description: The receive() function in message.jl:51 converts every parsed JSON3.Object to a Dict{String,Any} via a generator comprehension, allocating a new dict and String keys for every field on every inbound message. JSON3 is a lazy parser that avoids allocations internally; immediately discarding the lazy representation wastes that benefit. Consider threading JSON3.Object (or a typed wrapper) through the middleware stack using AbstractDict to avoid the copy. Candidate benchmark: @allocated receive(transport) to measure baseline allocation vs JSON3.Object-preserving path.

title: perf: per-eval @async stdin feeder Task + Pipe allocation on named-session hot path
description: For named-session evals with allow_stdin=true (the default path), eval_responses (eval.jl:317) creates a new Base.Pipe (two libuv handles) and spawns an @async feeder Task on every eval. The feeder task is interrupted but not awaited in the finally block. This is steady per-eval heap allocation of two libuv handles + Task + closure for every interactive eval. Consider reusing a persistent feeder task and pipe across evals within a named session rather than recreating them each time.

title: perf: @async timeout task + closure heap allocation per eval (task churn)
description: eval_responses (eval.jl:277) spawns a new @async Task for every eval with a timeout configured. The closure boxes ch, eval_task, timed_out, and effective_timeout_ms. The timedwait polls every 50ms. With default timeout of 30s and max_concurrent_evals=10, up to 10 polling tasks run simultaneously. Under high eval rate, this creates continuous task churn and GC pressure. Consider a shared timer wheel or cooperative timer pool that reuses tasks rather than allocating one per eval.

title: LoadFileMiddleware, CompleteMiddleware, LookupMiddleware absent from default_middleware_stack
description: src/middleware/core.jl default_middleware_stack() returns only SessionMiddleware, SessionOpsMiddleware, DescribeMiddleware, InterruptMiddleware, StdinMiddleware, EvalMiddleware, UnknownOpMiddleware. LoadFileMiddleware, CompleteMiddleware, and LookupMiddleware are fully implemented, exported, and tested but excluded. Any user relying on serve() with defaults cannot access load-file, complete, or lookup ops without building a custom stack. This is a silent capability gap not documented in README or serve() docstring. Fix: either add the three middleware to the default stack, or prominently document that callers must pass a custom stack to access these ops.

title: clients and client_tasks vectors in TCPServerHandle mutated from multiple tasks without a lock
description: src/transport/tcp.jl TCPServerHandle.clients and client_tasks are plain Vector fields. The accept loop pushes to both vectors; each client @async task calls filter! on both in its finally block. Julia Vector is not concurrent-safe. Under multi-threading this is a data race; under cooperative scheduling it is fragile (filter! reallocates in-place while another task may be iterating or pushing). Fix: protect both vectors with a ReentrantLock, or replace them with a thread-safe collection (e.g. a Channel or a locked wrapper).

title: AuditLog is implemented but record_audit! is never called in production paths
description: src/security/audit.jl implements AuditLog with record_audit!, eviction, file rotation, and entry serialization. Unit tests verify isolation. However, no middleware, server, or transport code calls record_audit! in any production path. The audit subsystem is effectively dead code at runtime. Fix: wire record_audit! into EvalMiddleware (at minimum) and/or create an AuditMiddleware that intercepts all request/response pairs.

title: EVAL_IO_CAPTURE_LOCK serializes all evals globally, defeating max_concurrent_evals
description: src/middleware/eval.jl EVAL_IO_CAPTURE_LOCK is a module-level ReentrantLock held during the entire redirect_stdout+eval execution. All concurrent evals across all sessions are serialized on this single lock. max_concurrent_evals is enforced before acquiring this lock, so the configured concurrency limit is never actually achieved. Fix: explore per-process pipe-based IO capture that does not require a global lock (e.g. using mktemp IOBuffers that do not use dup2), or document the serialization as a hard constraint and adjust max_concurrent_evals defaults accordingly.

title: max_output_bytes and max_session_history ResourceLimits fields are not enforced at runtime
description: ResourceLimits.max_output_bytes and max_session_history are documented as 'Enforcement deferred'. max_output_bytes is never checked against buffered IOBuffer content. clamp_history! reads the module-level MAX_SESSION_HISTORY_SIZE constant rather than the session's configured limit. Users configuring these fields via ResourceLimits() have no runtime effect. Fix: wire max_output_bytes check into _run_eval_core's IOBuffer drain, and pass the configured limit into clamp_history!.

title: Fix: max_sessions enforcement is non-atomic (MATH-1)
description:
Why: The check total_session_count < max_sessions in SessionMiddleware/SessionOpsMiddleware is not atomic with session creation. Two concurrent creators can both pass the check and exceed the configured limit, violating the resource-limit invariant.

Scope: Move session count check and creation into a single critical section under manager.lock in manager.jl. Use atomic check-then-insert semantics.

Found during: REPLy_jl-umg.10 formal-verification readiness pass.
Files: src/middleware/session.jl, src/middleware/session_ops.jl

title: Pass: synthesize findings into a remediation plan and implementation backlog
description:
Why
The review program is only useful if the outputs become an actionable remediation plan.

Scope
- All review artifacts created under this epic

Expected outcomes
- Consolidated risk register
- Prioritized remediation plan
- Implementation backlog with dependencies
- Explicit calls for Openspec work where required

Workflow
This is a planning/synthesis ticket. No code changes; produce implementation-ready work items.

## Required Skills
create-plan,create-issues,issue-review,context-guardian
acceptance_criteria:
- A consolidated findings summary exists
- Findings are grouped into implementation themes with priorities and dependencies
- Follow-up tickets are implementation-ready and use TDD/Tidy First language
- The backlog distinguishes review debt from product/spec change work
notes:
Execution mode
- Type: AFK

Artifact convention
- Record the final output with wai as a research artifact tied to this project.
- Include the issue ID in the artifact title or opening line.

title: Add wire protocol reference page to docs
description:
## Why this issue exists

A Julia developer writing a copy-paste client (the intended onboarding path per design decision) has no single reference page for the REPLy wire protocol. The information exists but is scattered:
- Request shape: Quick Start (one example, happy path only)
- Success response shape: openspec/specs/
- Error response shape: session status spec / smoke test
- 'done' status: inferred from smoke test

There is no single scannable page that shows: request → success response → error response → done signal.

## What needs to be done

Create a **Protocol Reference** page in docs/src/ that covers:
1. Request envelope shape (JSON fields: op, code, session, uuid)
2. Success response shape (status: done, result fields)
3. Error response shape (status: error, err, ex, stacktrace fields)
4. Session not found / path not allowed status flags (per the existing spec)
5. At least one worked example showing request + success response + error response side by side

The page should be linked from the Quick Start and the custom client tutorial.

## Acceptance criteria
- A developer can find the complete request/response contract without reading source code
- Error handling section shows the exact JSON fields a client must check
- Page is reachable from Quick Start and custom client tutorial

title: Fix Quick Start to link custom client tutorial and clarify copy-paste recipe workflow
description:
## Problem

The Quick Start ends at a bare 'nc + JSON' example with no bridge to persistent editor integration. A Julia developer following the docs has no signal that:
1. The nc example is for exploration only
2. The intended persistent-integration path is to copy the custom client recipe
3. The custom client tutorial exists at all

The custom client tutorial itself opens by defining a 'module REPLyClient' without any 'copy this file into your project' instruction — it reads like a library import, not a copy-paste recipe.

## What needs to change

1. Quick Start: add a final paragraph or note bridging to the custom client tutorial
   - 'The nc example works for exploration; for persistent editor integration, follow [custom client tutorial]'
2. custom-client.md: add a 'Copy this file' instruction at the top, clarify this is a recipe not a package
3. Verify no install/Pkg.add blocks in the tutorial imply it's a registered package

## Acceptance criteria
- A Julia developer who reads Quick Start can find the persistent-integration path without searching
- The custom client tutorial's first visible instruction is 'copy this file into your project'
- No text in either doc implies REPLyClient is a published package dependency

title: Fix outdated sessions how-to: client can create sessions via new-session op
description:
The sessions how-to incorrectly states 'creating a named session must be done from Julia when instantiating the server.' This is false — the implementation exposes a fully client-callable new-session RPC op (src/middleware/session_ops.jl:88-116).

**What's wrong:**
- How-to frames the entire tutorial around a server-side-only constraint that no longer exists
- Missing docs for client-callable session ops: new-session, ls-sessions, clone, close
- Quick Start and sessions how-to don't cross-reference each other, so readers hit the constraint as a surprise after following Quick Start
- No mention that all Quick Start evals are stateless by default

**What needs to change:**
- Rewrite the sessions how-to opening — remove the server-side-only constraint framing
- Document the four client-callable RPC ops (new-session, ls-sessions, clone, close) with examples
- Add a callout in Quick Start pointing to the sessions how-to so readers know persistence requires session setup
- Clarify the server-side create_named_session! function's role (seed sessions at startup, not the primary interface)

**Primary audience:** Julia developers wanting a persistent REPL for editor workflows.

title: Audit and specify MCP adapter session handling
description: Why this exists: The Phase 10 spec audit flagged MCP adapter session handling as a MEDIUM gap with partial coverage. The audit found src/mcp_adapter.jl exists but session handling completeness is unknown. This ticket exists to prevent silent drift between the MCP adapter and the hybrid session identity model being introduced in REPLy_jl-awg. What needs to be done: (1) Review src/mcp_adapter.jl against the hybrid identity spec (awg) to identify any session-reference patterns that use names instead of UUIDs. (2) Update the adapter to use spec-compliant session references. (3) Verify that the MCP adapter e2e coverage includes session creation and reuse. Source files: src/mcp_adapter.jl.

title: Implement Revise.jl pre-eval hook (REQ-RPL-060)
description:
Why this exists: The spec (REQ-RPL-060) requires a pre-eval hook that calls Revise.revise() before each eval when Revise.jl is loaded in the session. This ensures code changes are picked up automatically in long-running sessions. The hook is currently not implemented. What needs to be done: (1) Before each eval execution, check if Revise is loaded (isdefined(Main, :Revise)). (2) If loaded, call Revise.revise() and catch any errors (log them but do not abort the eval). (3) Add a config option to disable this hook (opt-out) for performance-sensitive use cases — config key: 'revise_hook_enabled' (boolean, default true) in server config. (4) Add unit and e2e tests.

Test files: test/unit/revise_hook_test.jl (unit tests for hook logic in isolation), test/e2e/revise_hook_test.jl (integration test verifying Revise is called before eval in a live server).

Source files: src/middleware/eval.jl (pre-eval hook invocation).

title: Add interrupt-id to interrupt op for per-eval targeting
description:
Why this exists: The spec requires the interrupt operation to accept an optional 'interrupt-id' to target a specific in-flight eval rather than all evals in a session. The interrupted response should return the eval ID that was interrupted. The current implementation only supports session-level interrupt and returns the session name. What needs to be done: (1) Assign a unique eval-id to every submitted eval — use a monotonic integer counter per-session (simpler than UUIDs, sufficient for targeting a specific in-flight eval). (2) Return eval-id in the eval response. (3) Accept optional 'interrupt-id' in interrupt request to target a specific eval. (4) Return 'interrupted-id' (the integer eval ID) in the interrupted response. (5) Update e2e tests for interrupt.

Response schemas: eval response includes {'eval-id':<int>,...}; interrupt request {'op':'interrupt','session':'<uuid-or-name>','interrupt-id':<int>}; interrupted response {'status':'interrupted','interrupted-id':<int>}.

Source files: src/middleware/interrupt.jl (interrupt handler), src/middleware/eval.jl (eval-id assignment and return).

title: Truncate eval repr output beyond a configurable threshold
description: EDGE-003: Eval responses currently call repr(value) without an output-size cap, so printing a very large array/string can allocate unbounded memory and produce oversized replies. Add a configurable repr/output threshold in the eval path and truncate output beyond that threshold with an explicit marker. Files: src/middleware/core.jl:74-85 (value formatting and response construction), relevant eval middleware tests. Chosen approach: cap the serialized output returned to the client and include a visible truncation marker instead of attempting full rendering.
acceptance_criteria: Done when: (1) evaluating an expression with a huge display representation returns a truncated string with a marker, (2) normal small values are unaffected, and (3) regression tests cover both cases.

title: Validate named session names at middleware boundaries

Files: src/middleware/session_ops.jl:41-87 (close-session / clone-session parameter validation), src/middleware/session.jl or other request-routing entry points that accept named-session identifiers, plus session operation tests. Chosen approach: centralize validation in a helper used by the middleware boundary so invalid names are rejected before registry lookup or mutation.
acceptance_criteria: Done when: (1) invalid names such as empty/whitespace-only or names containing disallowed characters are rejected with a clear error, (2) valid names continue to work, and (3) tests cover both acceptance and rejection cases.

title: Limit inbound TCP message size and reject oversized requests
description: EDGE-003: The TCP transport currently accepts arbitrarily large JSON messages, which can exhaust memory before request handling begins. Implement a configurable maximum message size at the transport boundary and reject oversized requests with a structured JSON-RPC error while keeping the server process healthy. Files: src/transport/tcp.jl (message read loop / request intake), test/e2e or test/integration transport coverage. Chosen approach: enforce the limit while reading from the socket rather than after full buffering, so the server never materializes an unbounded payload in memory.
acceptance_criteria: Done when: (1) a request just under the limit is processed normally, (2) a request over the limit returns a clear error response or clean disconnect per protocol choice, and (3) a regression test proves the limit is enforced in src/transport/tcp.jl.

title: Add timeout to collect_reply_stream and close_server!
description: EDGE-003/EDGE-004: collect_reply_stream at src/mcp_adapter.jl:138 and close_server! at src/transport/tcp.jl have no timeouts. A stuck eval or unresponsive connection blocks indefinitely. Fix: add configurable timeout (default 30s) to collect_reply_stream using Timer/timedwait. Add a timeout to close_server! that force-closes after grace period. Cross-ref: overlaps with REPLy_jl-v3f which covers broader timeout/disconnect cleanup — this ticket adds the basic timeout mechanism, v3f adds comprehensive disconnect handling. Files: src/mcp_adapter.jl:138 (collect_reply_stream), src/transport/tcp.jl (close_server!). Done when: a test verifies that a stuck eval times out after the configured period and returns a timeout error response.
close_reason: Implemented: configurable timeout in collect_reply_stream (timedwait, task-local collection) and close_server! (grace_seconds). 811 tests pass.

title: Implement Unix Domain Socket transport
Clarify and harden malformed JSON behavior at the TCP boundary
description:
## Context
The malformed-JSON contract is currently ambiguous between `.wai/projects/tracer-bullet/research/2026-04-17-tracer-bullet-scope-a-tracer-bullet-cuts-throu.md` and `.wai/projects/tracer-bullet/designs/2026-04-17-testing-validation-strategy-principle-o.md`, while the implementation currently causes the transport to return nothing and the connection to end quietly.

This issue is intentionally independent of the eval-streaming work because it concerns the TCP boundary before normal handler execution.

## Files
- src/protocol/message.jl
- src/transport/tcp.jl
- test/unit/message_test.jl
- test/e2e/
- .wai/projects/tracer-bullet/research/2026-04-17-tracer-bullet-scope-a-tracer-bullet-cuts-throu.md
- .wai/projects/tracer-bullet/designs/2026-04-17-testing-validation-strategy-principle-o.md

## Done
- [ ] The project chooses one explicit contract for malformed wire JSON: structured protocol error response or intentional connection close.
- [ ] The chosen contract is recorded in issue notes before implementation changes land.
- [ ] Unit/e2e tests cover the chosen behavior.
- [ ] Code and tracer-bullet docs agree on the behavior.
- [ ] `just --justfile justfile test` passes.

## Workflow Mandate
- Resolve the contract before changing implementation.
- Record the chosen malformed-JSON contract in issue notes first, then add failing tests, then implement, then align docs.
- Keep scope focused on malformed JSON at the TCP boundary only.
notes: Chosen malformed-JSON contract: treat malformed wire JSON as a closed transport boundary. The server sends no structured protocol error because request parsing never yields a valid id envelope; it closes the connection quietly after the malformed line. This preserves the existing receive(::JSONTransport) -> nothing contract and keeps malformed framing behavior aligned with EOF/partial-read handling under REQ-RPL-040b.
